edges.description: Destabilization of the holoenzyme yields a catalytically deficient Complex IV.
edges.source: COXFA4/NDUFA4 Subunit Loss and Failed Complex IV Assembly
edges.target: Impaired Terminal Electron Transfer and ATP Synthesis

edges.description: Impaired oxidative ATP synthesis forces anaerobic glycolysis, raising lactate.
edges.source: Impaired Terminal Electron Transfer and ATP Synthesis

edges.description: The bioenergetic deficit injures high-demand CNS tissue, producing the Leigh-syndrome neurological phenotype.
edges.source: Impaired Terminal Electron Transfer and ATP Synthesis

edges.description: Systemic metabolic decompensation compounds the energy deficit in the CNS.
CNS energy failure manifests as psychomotor/developmental delay.
Bilateral white-matter injury manifests as leukoencephalopathy on neuroimaging.

nodes.description: Biallelic loss-of-function variants in COXFA4 (formerly NDUFA4) abolish steady-state levels of the COXFA4/NDUFA4 polypeptide, a nuclear-encoded subunit of cytochrome c oxidase. Although the protein was long attributed to Complex I, it is a stoichiometric component of the individual COX complex, and its loss destabilizes the holoenzyme, producing an isolated Complex IV biogenesis defect rather than a Complex I deficiency.
nodes.id: COXFA4/NDUFA4 Subunit Loss and Failed Complex IV Assembly

biological_processes: mitochondrial respiratory chain complex IV assembly

evidence.reference_title: NDUFA4 mutations underlie dysfunction of a cytochrome c oxidase subunit linked to human neurological disease.
evidence.snippet: affected individuals harbored homozygous splice donor site mutations in NDUFA4, a gene previously assigned to encode a mitochondrial respiratory chain complex I (NADH:ubiquinone oxidoreductase) subunit.

evidence.reference_title: NDUFA4 mutations underlie dysfunction of a cytochrome c oxidase subunit linked to human neurological disease.
evidence.snippet: Western blot analysis of denaturing gels and immunocytochemistry revealed undetectable steady-state NDUFA4 protein levels, indicating that the mutation causes a loss-of-function effect in the homozygous state.

evidence.reference_title: NDUFA4 (Renamed COXFA4) Is a Cytochrome-c Oxidase Subunit.
evidence.snippet: it was demonstrated that NDUFA4, a polypeptide previously attributed to mitochondrial Complex I, was a 14th subunit of COX.

nodes.description: Psychomotor (developmental) delay from early childhood, part of the Leigh-syndrome presentation of COXFA4/NDUFA4 deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Biallelic NDUFA4 Deletion Causes Mitochondrial Complex IV Deficiency in a Patient with Leigh Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: a 4-year-old female presenting from early childhood with psychomotor delay

nodes.description: The bioenergetic deficit manifests predominantly in the central nervous system, the highest-energy-demand tissue, producing a Leigh-syndrome phenotype: subacute psychomotor delay/regression with bilateral, often brainstem-involving, white-matter signal changes. The two reported families had a relatively mild Leigh syndrome course.

evidence.reference_title: NDUFA4 mutations underlie dysfunction of a cytochrome c oxidase subunit linked to human neurological disease.
evidence.snippet: a consanguineous pedigree with isolated COX deficiency linked to a Leigh syndrome neurological phenotype

evidence.reference_title: Biallelic NDUFA4 Deletion Causes Mitochondrial Complex IV Deficiency in a Patient with Leigh Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: dysfunction of NDUFA4 was previously documented as causing mitochondrial Complex IV deficiency nuclear type 21 (MC4DN21, OMIM 619065), a relatively mild form of Leigh syndrome.

nodes.description: Loss of COXFA4/NDUFA4 reduces the amount and activity of assembled Complex IV, blocking electron transfer from cytochrome c to molecular oxygen and the coupled proton pumping that drives oxidative ATP synthesis. Complex IV activity was significantly reduced in patient-derived cells.
nodes.id: Impaired Terminal Electron Transfer and ATP Synthesis

biological_processes: mitochondrial electron transport, cytochrome c to oxygen

evidence.reference_title: Biallelic NDUFA4 Deletion Causes Mitochondrial Complex IV Deficiency in a Patient with Leigh Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: NDUFA4 encodes for a subunit of the respiratory chain Complex IV, whose activity was significantly reduced in the patient's fibroblasts.

nodes.description: The block in oxidative phosphorylation shifts energy production toward anaerobic glycolysis, generating lactic acidosis, a characteristic biochemical hallmark of the disorder reported in an affected patient.

evidence.reference_title: Biallelic NDUFA4 Deletion Causes Mitochondrial Complex IV Deficiency in a Patient with Leigh Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: psychomotor delay and white matter signal changes affecting several brain regions, including the brainstem, in addition to lactic and phytanic acidosis, compatible with Leigh syndrome

nodes.description: Bilateral white-matter signal changes affecting several brain regions, including the brainstem, on neuroimaging — the radiological hallmark of the Leigh-syndrome phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Biallelic NDUFA4 Deletion Causes Mitochondrial Complex IV Deficiency in a Patient with Leigh Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: white matter signal changes affecting several brain regions, including the brainstem
